author: Dr. Lisa Martinez
hpi: Mr. Morrison is a 72-year-old male with Type 2 DM (diagnosed 2010), diabetic peripheral neuropathy, CKD 3b, HFrEF, and persistent A-fib who presents for endocrinology follow-up focused on diabetes management. He started empagliflozin 10mg daily in May 2025 and is now approximately 3 months into therapy. He reports that his home glucose readings have improved notably — fasting glucoses averaging 120-135 mg/dL (previously 140-165) and post-prandial readings generally under 180 mg/dL. He has not experienced any hypoglycemic episodes. He has lost approximately 1.5 kg since starting empagliflozin (97.1 kg in August vs. 98.5 in April), which he is pleased about. He denies any genital itching, burning, or discharge. No urinary tract infections. He reports mildly increased urinary frequency, which is challenging given his existing BPH-related nocturia, but overall tolerable. His neuropathy symptoms are stable — bilateral foot burning and tingling rated 4/10, managed on gabapentin 300mg TID. He reports the burning is worse in the evenings and occasionally disrupts his sleep. He denies new numbness extending above the ankles. He had his annual eye exam in December 2023 which showed mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy — follow-up due later this year. He is compliant with podiatry visits. He has been adherent to a reduced-carbohydrate diet and checks his blood sugar twice daily (fasting and one post-meal).

reviewOfSystems.Constitutional: No fever, chills, or night sweats. Weight down ~1.5 kg. Energy level improved slightly from prior visits.
reviewOfSystems.Cardiovascular: Baseline dyspnea on exertion walking 2 blocks (improved from 1-2 blocks — may reflect empagliflozin benefit). No orthopnea, PND, chest pain, or palpitations.
reviewOfSystems.GI: Regular bowel movements. Appetite fair. No nausea, vomiting, melena, or abdominal pain. Compliant with pantoprazole.
reviewOfSystems.GU: Nocturia x2. Mild increase in daytime frequency since empagliflozin (expected). No dysuria, hematuria, genital symptoms, or UTI symptoms.
reviewOfSystems.Musculoskeletal: Chronic low back stiffness. No acute complaints.
reviewOfSystems.Neurologic: Bilateral foot burning and tingling 4/10, stable. Worse in evenings. No new numbness above ankles. No new weakness.
reviewOfSystems.Endocrine: No polydipsia beyond usual. No symptoms suggestive of DKA (no nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, confusion). No heat/cold intolerance.
reviewOfSystems.Skin: No foot ulcers. Skin intact on bilateral feet per patient report. No genital rashes.

physicalExam.General: Alert, oriented, well-developed overweight male in no acute distress. Pleasant, cooperative. BMI approximately 32.
physicalExam.HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. PERRL. Fundoscopic exam: unable to adequately visualize retina in office (dilated exam deferred to ophthalmology). Oropharynx clear. Mucous membranes moist. No thyromegaly or nodules.
physicalExam.Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular rhythm (A-fib), rate 80. S1/S2 present. Soft systolic murmur at apex. No gallops. JVP not elevated.
physicalExam.Respiratory: Mild bibasilar crackles, chronic. No wheezes or rhonchi.
physicalExam.Abdomen: Soft, obese, non-tender, non-distended. No organomegaly.
physicalExam.Extremities: Trace bilateral ankle edema. Feet examined: skin intact, no ulcerations, no calluses, no interdigital maceration. Toenails mildly thickened (onychomycosis, left great toe). Dorsalis pedis pulses 1+ bilaterally. Posterior tibial pulses 1+ bilaterally.
physicalExam.Neurologic: Alert and oriented x3. Monofilament testing: 3/10 sites detected right foot, 4/10 sites detected left foot (reduced from normal 8-10/10 — moderate sensory loss). Vibration sense absent at bilateral great toes, diminished at bilateral medial malleoli. Deep tendon reflexes: ankles 0/4 bilaterally (absent), knees 1+/4 bilaterally (diminished). No ulnar or median nerve findings.
physicalExam.Skin: No acanthosis nigricans. No necrobiosis lipoidica. Skin dry bilateral lower extremities with mild xerosis.

assessment.diagnosis: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with diabetic neuropathy
assessment.notes: HbA1c improved to 7.2% (from 7.4% in June, 7.8% in October 2023) — best result in several years. Empagliflozin providing significant glycemic benefit. Fasting and post-prandial glucoses improved. No hypoglycemia. Weight loss of 1.5 kg likely from SGLT2i glycosuric effect. Current regimen: metformin 500mg BID, insulin glargine 24 units QHS, empagliflozin 10mg daily.

Stable clinically (pain 4/10, burning worse evenings). Monofilament testing shows moderate sensory loss (3-4/10 sites). Vibration sense absent at toes. Absent ankle reflexes. Gabapentin 300mg TID with partial benefit. If symptoms worsen, could consider pregabalin but must coordinate with cardiology given fluid retention risk. Fall risk is significant due to impaired proprioception.
Diagnosed December 2023. No macular edema at that time. Follow-up dilated exam due December 2024. Improved glycemic control should help slow progression.

eGFR 34.8 last check (August labs). Empagliflozin expected initial hemodynamic dip in eGFR — Cr 2.00 at 2 weeks post-start in May, now stabilizing. Long-term renal protection expected. UACR to be rechecked.

Incidental finding. Mild thickening of left great toenail. Diabetic patients at higher risk for secondary infections from nail dystrophy. Topical antifungal preferred over oral given CKD and polypharmacy.

plan.action: Excellent progress — A1c 7.2%, best in years. Continue current triple therapy: metformin 500mg BID, insulin glargine 24 units QHS, empagliflozin 10mg daily. Do NOT reduce insulin yet — want to maintain trajectory. If A1c drops below 7.0% with any hypoglycemia symptoms, will consider reducing glargine. Continue home glucose monitoring: fasting AM and one random post-prandial daily. Target fasting 80-130, post-prandial <180. Counseled on sick-day rules for SGLT2i: hold empagliflozin during acute illness, surgery, or fasting to reduce euglycemic DKA risk.

Stable on gabapentin 300mg TID. Pain 4/10, tolerable. If worsening, next step would be pregabalin (coordinate with cardiology regarding fluid retention) or duloxetine (if depression management also desired — coordinate with PCP regarding mood assessment). Reinforce daily foot inspection. Protective footwear with orthotics. Continue podiatry visits q3-4 months.
Schedule ophthalmology appointment for dilated exam in December 2024. Improved A1c should help slow progression.
Start ciclopirox 8% topical nail lacquer to left great toenail daily. Monitor for improvement over 6-12 months. Avoid oral terbinafine given CKD and hepatic steatosis.
Well tolerated at 3 months. No genital infections or UTIs. Continue at 10mg daily. Monitor renal function, potassium, and volume status at regular intervals.
Return in 3 months (November 2024) for A1c recheck. Labs: A1c, BMP, UACR before next visit. Coordinate with nephrology and cardiology for comprehensive metabolic management.

attestation:
I personally saw and examined the patient including comprehensive diabetic foot examination. I agree with the documented findings and plan.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Lisa Martinez, MD
Date: 8/8/2024
